title: Social Credit: Scores At Long Last?
text:
“C omprehensive enterprise credit assessments ” may be the most meaningful form of score or grade in China’s social credit system, but they bear little resemblance to the all-encompassing and all-defining citizen-ranking “social credit scores” that once dominated the foreign  imagination  and  media .
Businesses, not individuals, have always been the main focus of social credit. Yet the myth of citizen scoring was so pervasive that some observers even coined the term “ corporate social credit system ” to refer to the real regulatory framework, just to distinguish it from the fantasy.
Today, the mythos of the citizen scoring panopticon has been largely dispelled and lingers mainly as  a social media trope , but few people are aware of what China’s Social Credit System actually looks like. Reviewing a  new plan for improving the Comprehensive Enterprise Credit Status Assessment System  is an opportunity to help correct that.
1. BACKGROUND
Throughout its development, social credit has generally included three main pillars:
Credit Regulation (  信用监管) , also called “Credit-based Regulation”, primarily refers to the idea that government agencies should treat regulatory subjects differently based on their record of compliance with laws and regulations. This means increasing scrutiny of those with past violations, and offering facilitated services to those in good standing.
Credit Reporting   (征信),  like most familiar financial credit systems, is concerned with measuring individuals’ likelihood of defaulting on their loans and contractual obligations. To encourage credit lending, China hopes to create a well-regulated system of private  credit reporting  institutions that offer assessments of businesses’ and individuals’ financial credit.
Creditworthiness  (诚信)  is a piece of China’s ubiquitous ‘Core Socialist Values’ morality campaign, and seeks to foster honesty and integrity among citizens, organizations, and government agencies.
2. Credit Assessments
Basic Structure:
At the highest level, the plan separates two basic types of assessment:  public credit assessments and market credit assessments.
In late 2019,  public credit assessments  for businesses were advanced  as a key component of the Credit Regulation framework . The new implementation plan discusses the relationship between public and market credit assessments, and in doing so helps clarify the relationship between the Credit Regulation and Credit Reporting systems.
In the social credit context, the public/market distinction has previously been used to distinguish between the types of information used to determine a subject’s credit status:
Public Credit Information  is defined as information produced or acquired by state authorities (or authorized organizations) in the performance of their legally-prescribed duties. This includes information on any licenses or permits granted to a subject, or penalties for misconduct).
In 2021, the central government began regularly publishing official  Basic Catalogs of Public Credit Information  to be used in credit regulation.
Market Credit Information , sometimes called “non-public credit information”, is a broader category of information collected by private entities such as credit service institutions.
A. PUBLIC CREDIT ASSESSMENTS  are primarily based on public credit information. They are described as being created by government agencies to reflect a subject’s compliance with laws and regulations, and are used as a basis for regulation. In short, they are at the heart of Credit Regulation as described above.
Public Credit Assessments are themselves divided into two categories:
Industry Public Credit Assessments   – are sector-specific measurements of compliance conducted by the various regulatory authorities for use within their jurisdiction. In line with the concept of Credit Regulation, industry regulators are to treat enterprises differently based on these assessments of past compliance with the relevant laws and regulations.
Table 1: Overview of Credit Assessment Types
The central government regulatory bodies for each sector are responsible for making rules for that sector’s industry assessments. They are instructed to include indexes of information to be considered, the procedures, and potential uses of the results. If a central authority has not yet made rules, the local regulatory authorities under it are generally precluded from creating and implementing their own credit assessments without first seeking approval.
Comprehensive Public Credit     Assessments    are an attempt to measure entities’ more global legal compliance. The National Development and Reform Commission (NDRC) is responsible for developing the rules for these assessments.
Inputs and Outputs
The   inputs   for both types of Public Credit Assessment are usually limited to information from the  Public Credit Information Catalog , but can include other relevant information acquired or generated by government agencies when performing their duties.  A catalog of information for inclusion in comprehensive public credit assessments was provided with the plan.
The   output of public credit assessments   is to be a grade, usually on a 4-grade scale, of A, B, C, or D. If a more complicated point system is used, the government agency should disclose what score ranges translate to what grades. The broader rules for making assessments are also to be made public.
Assessment information is to be available to regulatory departments through the National Credit Information Sharing Platform. They may be made publicly available by the relevant government authority, either proactively or upon application. They will be available through both the Credit China website and the website of the department that made them, where they can be accessed by the subject of the report and others that they authorize.
Relationship of Comprehensive and Industry Public Credit Assessments
Comprehensive and Industry public credit assessments are intended to inform each other.
Naturally, the comprehensive assessments should reflect regulatory activity in individual sectors, and the relevant departments are asked to share information listed in the  assessment data director y   via the National Credit Information Sharing Platform.
At the same time, industry assessments are to include the comprehensive public credit assessment outcome as a factor, although the weight given to it may vary. The NDRC is to send comprehensive assessment outcomes to all relevant departments and regional governments.
One hard rule is that if an entity has a D rating in the comprehensive assessment, it cannot receive an A in any industry assessment. This means that a D in the comprehensive assessment precludes receiving preferential treatment in any sector.
B. MARKET CREDIT ASSESSMENTS  are designed to reflect a business’s reliability in market activities, and may be consulted in making lending and financing decisions.
They are usually produced by credit reporting institutions authorized (under the management of the People’s Bank of China) as commercially available products based on government data and judicial information, as well as information from industry associations, individual businesses, creditors, and business counterparts. The plan explicitly encourages the consideration of public credit assessments as a factor in market credit assessments.
Simply put, market assessments use public and market credit information to assess the likelihood of an entity defaulting on loans or contracts. In other words, market credit assessments are concerned with the  Credit Reporting  aspect of social credit.
The plan does not expressly divide market credit assessments into two categories, but does include a meaningful distinction: With the guidance of regulatory authorities, quasi-regulatory institutions such as Industry Associations and Chambers of Commerce may also carry out non-profit market credit assessments for use in industry discipline and management, so long as they do not treat members and non-members differently.
While this second type of market credit assessment is correctly categorized as a ‘market’ assessment because it is not produced by government authorities, it seems fundamentally distinct from those made by credit reporting institutions. All market credit assessments are defined by their focus on measuring the risk of default in market activities, but here that risk is to be used in industry discipline and management, a quasi-regulatory function. The required supervision of the regulatory authorities highlights the awkward fit, as those authorities’ expertise is limited to legal compliance rather than business conduct.
In context, this second type of market credit assessment is likely only meant to build on the expertise of industry associations and chambers of commerce regarding the corresponding local or sectoral business environments, allowing them to identify well-established, reliable businesses and those subject to frequent complaints. This is a potentially useful service for members and consumers that could help maintain industry standards and reputation, but it is worth highlighting as a distinct form of credit assessment.
C. Additional Applications of Credit Assessments
3. Remedies: Credit Repair and Assessment Objections
China has already been developing  a system for public credit repair,  the purging of records on non-compliance. Generally speaking, these rules require that information on violations remain publicly visible and available to be relied on in regulation for a minimum period, after which the impacted entity can apply to have it removed once they have remedied that problem. Once repaired, regulators, credit reporting institutions, etc. are all to remove the relevant information.
The Plan also includes processes for objecting to public credit assessment results. The procedures remain vague, but require a review by the department that made the assessment, with the possibility of a correction.
Objections can also be made to third-party institutions regarding either inaccurate or incomplete information provided by that entity, OR problems with the outcome of a market credit assessment they created. Again, the procedures remain vague, and there is no information on further remedies via government agencies or the courts. The rules do mention, however, that the third-party must not demand compensation for correcting or updating information.
4. Further Integration?
The new plan provides useful clarity on the direction of social credit assessments. The relationship between Public Credit Assessments and Market Credit Assessments is more clearly mapped onto the Credit Regulation and Credit Reporting framework than ever before, and there is new guidance on how the two interact, as discussed above.
The policy going forward is for public assessments to serve as a foundation for future integration with market credit assessments, to build a unified, comprehensive enterprise credit assessment. But this raises further questions:
First, to what end?  Public credit assessments are based on records of legal compliance and used in credit regulation to alert the authorities about the appropriate level of regulatory scrutiny. Market credit assessments purport to measure the risk of an entity defaulting in business activities based on their past market practice. But what is the function of the proposed integrated assessment?  Would the resulting A,B,C,D provide anything beyond what the existing assessments and data provide?
Second, what of “creditworthiness”?   Creditworthiness (诚信) is the most subjective of the three pillars of social credit, attempting to combine general morality, professional ethics, good faith dealings, and fulfilment of social responsibility. Social Credit documents addressing it have generally focused more on publicity and education rather than any form of enforcement. While some regions have nominally awarded models of creditworthiness, the law has been clear that punishments are only appropriate for violations of law, not ‘creditworthiness’ alone.
Still, more than a third of the text of  a draft Social Credit    Law  released  in 2022 focused on ‘creditworthiness’, and it seems unlikely to simply disappear. It has been suggested that indicators of creditworthiness could even be included in credit reporting’s market assessments of the risk of loan default, particularly for those who have no history of borrowing. While not much is needed in this area if creditworthiness is to play only an educational role, guidance on how such information can be used, either alone or in other assessments,  would be welcome.

Attachment II: Public Credit Assessment Data Catalog
#
Information Type
Shared Content
1
Judicial Information
Judgment Defaulter Information
Case number, enterprise name, name of the legal representative or responsible person, ID number/uniform social credit code, performance status of the judgment defaulter, specific circumstances judgment defaulter’s conduct, enforcement court, location, date of case filing, publication date
2
Contract Violation Information
Information on contract violations found through judicial procedures
Enterprise name, uniform social credit code, cause of action, case number, plaintiff name, plaintiff identification number (if any)/ uniform social credit code (if any), the defendants’ names, defendants’ identification numbers(if any)/uniform social credit code (if any), judgment, enforcing court, location, date of publication
Information on Untrustworthiness: Corporate Accounts in Arrears
Enterprise name, uniform social credit code, designation time, reason for designation, designation amount, designation department, performance status
3
Enterprise Registration Information
Basic Enterprise Information
Enterprise name, uniform social credit code, registered capital or paid-in capital, staff size, date of establishment, state of the enterprise’s foreign investment and branch establishment, changed information, etc.
Information on Entry into Lists on Irregular Businesses
Enterprise name, uniform social credit code, whether they are entered on the list of irregularities, the type of reason for entry on the list of irregularities, the date of entry on the list of irregularities.
4
Administrative Management Information
Administrative Permits
Enterprise name, uniform social credit code, administrative counterpart category, legal representative name, legal representative document type, legal representative document number, administrative permitting decision document name, administrative permitting decision document number, administrative permit category, administrative permit name, administrative permit number, administrative permit content, administrative permitting decision date, administrative permit validity period, administrative permit status, administrative organ name, administrative organ’s uniform social credit code, data providing unit
Administrative Punishments
Enterprise name, uniform social credit code, administrative counterpart category, name of legal representative, type of legal representative document, legal representative document number, name of administrative penalty decision document, administrative penalty decision document number, type of illegal conduct, facts of the violation, basis for administrative penalty, content of administrative penalty, amount of fine, amount of confiscated unlawful gains and confiscated property, name and number of suspended or revoked license, date of administrative penalty decision, validity period of administrative penalty, end date for display, name of administrative organ, uniform social credit code of administrative organ, data providing unit
5
Lists on Incentives for Trustworthiness
Class A taxpayers
Enterprise name, uniform social credit code, rating year, credit level, place of assessment
Enterprises with High-level Customs Certification
Chinese name of the enterprise, uniform social credit code, customs registration code (enterprise registration number), credit level,time of designation, business category
Other Sectors’ Lists of Incentives for Trustworthiness.
Enterprise name, uniform social credit code, registered address, assessment level, time of designation, designation validity period, etc.
6
Lists of Seriously Untrustworthy Entities
List of Entities with Serious Untrustworthiness Involving Enterprises
Enterprise name, uniform social credit code, registered address, date of entry, date of incident, basis for entry, decision-making organ, date of removal, etc.

信息种类
共享内容
1
司法信息
失信被执行人信息
案号、企业名称、法定代表人或者负责人姓名、身份证号码/统一社会信用代码、失信被执行人的履行情况、失信被执行人行为具体情形、执行法院、所在地、立案时间、发布时间
2
合同履约信息
经司法程序认定的违约信息

按程序认定的拖欠企业账款失信信息
企业名称、统一社会信用代码、认定时间、认定案由、认定金额、认定部门、履约情况
3
企业登记信息
企业基础信息
企业名称、统一社会信用代码、注册资金或实缴资本、人员规模、成立日期、企业对外投资及分支机构设立情况、变更信息等
列入经营异常名录信息
企业名称、统一社会信用代码、是否列入经营异常名录、列入经营异常名录原因类型、列入经营异常名录日期
4
行政管理信息
行政许可
企业名称、统一社会信用代码、行政相对人类别、法定代表人姓名、法定代表人证件类型、法定代表人证件号码、行政许可决定文书名称、行政许可决定文书号、行政许可类别、行政许可证书名称、行政许可编号、行政许可内容、行政许可决定日期、行政许可有效期、行政许可状态、行政机关名称、行政机关统一社会信用代码、数据提供单位
行政处罚
企业名称、统一社会信用代码、行政相对人类别、法定代表人姓名、法定代表人证件类型、法定代表人证件号码、行政处罚决定文书名称、行政处罚决定文书号、违法行为类型、违法事实、行政处罚依据、行政处罚内容、罚款金额、没收违法所得和没收物的金额、暂扣或者吊销证照名称及编号、行政处罚决定日期、行政处罚有效期、公示截止期、行政机关名称、行政机关统一社会信用代码、数据提供单位
5
守信激励名单
A级纳税人
企业名称、统一社会信用代码、评级年度、信用等级、评价地域
海关高级认证企业
企业中文名称、统一社会信用代码、海关注册编码（企业注册号）、信用等级、等级认定时间、经营类别
其他领域守信激励名单

6
严重失信主体名单
涉企严重失信主体名单
